coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent carbon quantum dots synthesized by chemical vapor deposition: An alternative candidate for electron acceptor in polymer solar cells
coredata.dc:creator: Cui, Bo
Yan, Lingpeng
Gu, Huimin
Yang, Yongzhen
Liu, Xuguang
Ma, Chang-Qi
Chen, Yongkang
Jia, Husheng
coredata.dc:description: Excitation-wavelength-dependent blue-greenish fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with graphite structure were synthesized by chemical vapor deposition (CVD) method. In comparison with those synthesized by hydrothermal method (named H-CQDs), C-CQDs have less hydrophilic terminal groups, showing good solubility in common organic solvents. Furthermore, these synthesized C-CQDs show a low LUMO energy level (LUMO = −3.84 eV), which is close to that of phenyl-C61-butyric acid methyl ester (PC61BM, LUMO = −4.01 eV), the most widely used electron acceptor in polymer solar cells. Photoluminescence quenching of the poly(3-hexylthiophene-2,5-diyl):C-CQDs blended film (P3HT:C-CQDs) indicated that a photo-induced charge transfer between P3HT and C-CQDs occurs in such a composite film. Bulk heterojunction solar cells using C-CQDs as electron acceptors or doping materials were fabricated and tested. High fill factors were achieved for these C-CQDs based polymer solar cells, demonstrating that CQDs synthesized by CVD could be alternative to the fullerene derivatives for applying in polymer solar cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Polymer solar cells
Electron acceptor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of P3HT:C-CQDs based composite films and photovoltaic performance
Characterization of P3HT:C-CQDs:PC61BM based composite films and photovoltaic performance

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM (a) and HR-TEM (b) images of synthesized C-CQDs. (a) TEM images of synthesized C-CQDs. (b) HR-TEM images of synthesized C-CQDs [38].
FTIR spectra of C-CQDs and H-CQDs, Inset: dispersion images of C-CQDs in chlorobenzene (CB), 1,2-dichlorobenzene (DCB), toluene and water.
Static contact angles of diiodomethane and water on ITO coated with C-CQDs (a, c) and H-CQDs (b,d).

UV–Vis absorption spectra of C-CQDs in solution (a), PL spectra of C-CQDs excited at various wavelengths (b). (a) UV–Vis absorption spectra of C-CQDs in solution. (b) PL spectra of C-CQDs excited at various wavelengths.
CV curve of C-CQDs (1 mg/mL) in 0.1 M TBAP supported DMF, at a scan rate of 100 mV/s [38].

PL spectra of P3HT (10 mg/mL), C-CQDs (1 mg/mL) and P3HT:C-CQDs (P3HT: 10 mg/mL, C-CQDs content: 0, 2.5, 5, and 10 wt.% vs. P3HT) composite films at an excitation at 450 nm (a), UV–Vis absorption spectra of P3HT and P3HT:C-CQDs (1:5%) in solid state (b). (a) PL spectra of P3HT (10 mg/mL), C-CQDs (1 mg/mL) and P3HT:C-CQDs (P3HT: 10 mg/mL, C-CQDs content: 0, 2.5, 5, and 10 wt.% vs. P3HT) composite films at an excitation at 450 nm. (b) UV–Vis absorption spectra of P3HT and P3HT:C-CQDs (1:5%) in solid state.
Energy level diagram of the inverted solar cells with a structure of ITO/ZnO/P3HT:C-CQDs/MoO3/Al.

Dark (black line) and illuminated (red line) J–V curves of the devices based on pure P3HT and P3HT:C-CQDs (1:5%; w/w) (a), EQE spectra of corresponding devices (b). (a) Dark (black line) and illuminated (red line) J–V curves of the devices based on pure P3HT and P3HT:C-CQDs (1:5%; w/w). (b) EQE spectra of corresponding devices. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
FETEM image of P3HT:C-CQDs (a) and P3HT:H-CQDs (b) composite films (1:5%). (a) FETEM image of P3HT:C-CQDs composite films (1:5%). (b) FETEM image of P3HT:H-CQDs composite films (1:5%).

(a) J–V curves and (b) EQE spectra of the P3HT:C-CQDs:PC61BM based solar cell doped with different ration of C-CQDs. (a) J–V curves of the P3HT:C-CQDs:PC61BM based solar cell doped with different ration of C-CQDs. (b) EQE spectra of the P3HT:C-CQDs:PC61BM based solar cell doped with different ration of C-CQDs.
Characteristic parameters of P3HT:C-CQDs based BHJ solar cell.
Characteristic parameters of P3HT:C-CQDs:PC61BM based solar cell.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent carbon quantum dots synthesized by chemical vapor deposition: An alternative candidate for electron acceptor in polymer solar cells

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Interface Science and Engineering in Advanced Materials

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Excitation-wavelength-dependent blue-greenish fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with graphite structure were synthesized by chemical vapor deposition (CVD) method. In comparison with those synthesized by hydrothermal method (named H-CQDs), C-CQDs have less hydrophilic terminal groups, showing good solubility in common organic solvents. Furthermore, these synthesized C-CQDs show a low LUMO energy level (LUMO = −3.84 eV), which is close to that of phenyl-C61-butyric acid methyl ester (PC61BM, LUMO = −4.01 eV), the most widely used electron acceptor in polymer solar cells. Photoluminescence quenching of the poly(3-hexylthiophene-2,5-diyl):C-CQDs blended film (P3HT:C-CQDs) indicated that a photo-induced charge transfer between P3HT and C-CQDs occurs in such a composite film. Bulk heterojunction solar cells using C-CQDs as electron acceptors or doping materials were fabricated and tested. High fill factors were achieved for these C-CQDs based polymer solar cells, demonstrating that CQDs synthesized by CVD could be alternative to the fullerene derivatives for applying in polymer solar cells.

ce:section.ce:para: As a novel type of carbon nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted increasing interests after being obtained during purification of single-walled carbon nanotubes in 2004 [1]. CDs comprise discrete, quasi-spherical nanoparticles with sizes less than 10 nm [2]. There are two major types of CDs, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and carbon quantum dots (CQDs). GQDs are sheet-like materials consisting of single or few graphene layers, and usually show anisotropic charge transport properties in lateral dimension better than the vertical dimension. GQDs exhibit tunable fluorescence emission or electronic properties and low charge transfer resistance [3,4]. Generally, GQDs are obtained by two-step cutting routes from different carbon resources, such as carbon fiber [3], carbon nanotube [1] or graphene sheets [5,6]. Relatively complicated procedures and long synthesis periods would result in high cost of GQDs. As a zero-dimension nanocarbon materials, CQDs are usually spherical and have crystal graphite lattice [7,8]. CQDs can be synthesized by hydrothermal processes [9], ultrasonics- [10] or microwave-assistant [11,12] hydrothermal processes from cheap carbon sources. These hydrothermally synthesized CQDs (H-CQDs) usually contain multiple hydrophilic groups at the periphery, and have excellent solubility in polar solvent. In addition, H-CQDs possess advantages of excellent chemical and thermal stability, low toxicity, intensive photoluminescence performances [7], low photobleaching and good biocompatibility [13], and have been applied to fields of bioimaging [14,15] and labeling [16], drug delivery [17,18], sensing [14], fluorescent ink [19], photocatalysis [20,21] and light-emitting diode [22].

Recently, applications of CQDs in solar cells were also reported [23–25]. For example, Choi et al. [26] prepared carbon-dot-supported silver nanoparticles as the electrode in polymer solar cells. Such a composite electrode takes the surface plasmon resonance effect of metal particles, and power conversion efficiency enhancement was achieved. Narayanan et al. [27] and Liu et al. [28] reported the use of CQDs as an additive in quantum dot and organic solar cells, respectively. In these reports, charge transport as well as device performance was improved by cooperation with CQDs. Huang et al. reported the use of CQDs as a luminescent down-shifting layer on organic solar cells. Enhanced incident photon-to-current efficiency (IPCE) was observed over 350–450 nm, leading to a device performance enhancement [29]. Furthermore, Zhang et al. [30] and Briscoe et al. [31] reported the use of CQDs as a sensitizer in traditional liquid electrolyte and solid state dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs) respectively. Although all these CQD-based DSSCs showed low performance, these results confirmed that CQDs are promising optical active materials for use in DSSCs.

High photovoltaic performance of polymer solar cells based on CQD-doped photoactive layer has been reported in the literatures [32,33]. However, only few papers have reported the use of hydrothermally synthesized CQDs as a single electron acceptor in solution processed polymer solar cells. In 2014, Kwon et al. firstly reported the use of CQDs as electron acceptors in P3HT based solar cells, and a power conversion efficiency of 0.23% was reached [34]. Dhawan et al. reported the use of CQDs and ZnO:CQD composite as an electron acceptor in polymer solar cells, and high efficiency of more than 2% was achieved when they were blended with a large band-gap polymer [35]. It is worth to note that CQDs used in these polymer solar cells were synthesized by hydrothermal methods, which usually contain multiple hydrophilic terminal groups or with long alkyl side chains, which might hinder the charge transport in the blended film. Therefore, development of more controllable CQDs with less hydrophilic terminal groups is highly interesting for solution processed organic solar cells, since CQDs should have advantages of easy of large-scale preparation and low cost.

Knowing chemical vapor deposition (CVD) is an excellent alternative method to synthesize CQD with better property control and less hydrophilic groups, this paper report the synthesis of high quality CQDs via a simple and fast CVD method (named C-CQDs). Optical and electronic properties, as well as the use of C-CQDs in polymer solar cells were carefully investigated. The results showed that C-CQDs are applicable in polymer solar cells, promoting the development of high performance carbon based materials for polymer solar cells.

Highly pure C2H2 and Ar (>99.99%) were purchased from Leap Gas Co. Ltd. N,N-Dimethylformamide (DMF) was purchased from Tianjin Fuchen Chemical Reagents Factory. N-Hexane and cyclohexane were supplied by Tianjin Guangfu Technology Development Co. Ltd. Regioregular poly(3-hexylthiophene) was purchased from Solarmer Energy, Inc. (Beijing). ZnO was synthesized by so-gel method [36]. H-CQDs were synthesized by hydrothermal method [37].

C-CQDs were synthesized by CVD in Ar atmosphere using C2H2 as carbon source [38]. The preparation was conducted in a quartz tube (32 mm i.d. and 1 m long), which was mounted in a horizontal tubular furnace. At the beginning of synthesis, the quartz tube was heated to 1000 °C in Ar atmosphere. Then a mixture of C2H2/Ar was introduced into the reactor at a rate of 70/700 mL min−1 for 2 h. After the reaction, the quartz tube was cooled to room temperature under the protection of flowing Ar. The crude product was collected in a receiving flask, and dissolved in 200 mL of DMF by sonication for 30 min. The DMF solution was filtered through a filter paper (in middle speed) and a syringe filter (PTFE, 0.45 μm). The organic solvents were removed under vacuum to give C-CQDs.

Photovoltaic devices were fabricated on commercial glass slides coated with patterned ITO. The substrates were cleaned in an ultrasonic bath using detergent, deionized water, acetone and isopropanol in sequence. Immediately prior to device fabrication, the substrates were treated in a UV-ozone oven for 30 min. ZnO (10 mg/mL in acetone) was spin-coated on the cleaned and patterned ITO substrates at 2300 rpm for 60 s. The samples were subsequently annealed at 120 °C for 10 min on a hot plate. A solution of 10 mg/mL poly(3-hexylthiophene-2,5-diyl) (P3HT) in 1,2-dichlorobenzene with different CQDs contents (0, 2.5, 5 and 10 wt.% vs. P3HT) was spin-coated onto the top of the ZnO layer at 1000 rpm for 60 s and subsequently annealed at 120 °C for 10 min in order to fabricate P3HT:C-CQDs solar cells,. For P3HT:C-CQDs:PC61BM solar cells, the P3HT and PC61BM were first dissolved in 1,2-dichlorobenzene at a weight ratio of 1:1 (with a total concentration of 40 mg/mL) doping with different ratio of C-CQDs (0, 0.025, 0.05, 0.075 and 0.1 wt vs. P3HT:PC61BM). The wet P3HT:PC61BM blend films were then put into covered Petri dishes for 1.5 h. The samples were subsequently heated at 120 °C for 10 min. Finally, MoO3 (20 nm) and Al (100 nm) were sequentially vacuum-deposited on the top of the active layer as the hole-extracting layer and the anode, respectively. The effective area of the devices was 0.09 cm2.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images of the C-CQDs were taken from an FEI Tecani G2 F20 S-Twin 200 kV microscope. Fourier transformation infrared spectra of C-CQDs and H-CQDs were characterized by a Tenror 27 FTIR spectrometer. The U-3900 ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer (UV–Vis) and Fluoromax-4 photoluminescence (PL) spectrometer were used to characterize the optical property of the composite. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves are obtained in a tetrabutylammonium hexafluorophosphate (TBAPF6, 0.1 mol L−1) supported DMF solution at room temperature using a RST3000 electrochemical workstation operated at a scanning rate of 100 mV s−1, in which a Pt wire (ϕ = 1 mm) embedded in Teflon column was used as the working electrode, and a Pt sheet and Ag/AgCl electrodes were served as the counter and reference electrodes, respectively, and a ferrocene/ferrocenium was used as the internal reference to calibrate the redox potentials. The current density–voltage (J–V) characteristics are measured in a N2-filled glove box using a Keithley 2400 source meter under an AM 1.5G filter (100 mW cm2) generated by white light from a halogen tungsten lamp, filtered by a Schott GG385 UV filter and a Hoya LB120 daylight filter. External quantum efficiency (EQE) was measured under simulated one sun operation condition using bias light from a 532 nm solid state laser. Light from a 150 W tungsten halogen lamp (Osram 64610) was used as the probe light and modulated with a mechanical chopper before passing the monochromator (Zolix, Omni-k300) to select the wavelength. The response was recorded as the voltage by an I-V converter (DNR-IV Convertor, Suzhou D&R Instruments), using a lock-in amplifier (Stanford Research Systems SR 830). A calibrated Si cell was used as reference. The device for EQE measurement was kept behind a quartz window in a nitrogen filled container.

Fig. 1 shows TEM and HRTEM images of C-CQDs. As shown in Fig. 1(a), the synthesized C-CQDs have good monodispersion and narrow size distribution. According to the statistical particle size distribution (inset at the top left of Fig. 1(a)), the typical sizes of the C-CQDs are in the range of 2–7 nm, with an average diameter of 3.5 nm. C-CQDs show well-resolved lattice fringes with interplanar spacings of 0.20 and 0.27 nm (Fig. 1(b)), which are close to the (102) and (020) diffraction facets of sp2 graphite carbon [29,39], implying that these C-CQDs are structured like crystalline graphite carbon. Moreover, the electron diffraction pattern in an inset at the right-bottom corner of Fig. 1(b) also indicates crystalline nature of the C-CQDs.

The surface functional groups of C-CQDs were characterized by FTIR, and compared with H-CQDs. Fig. 2 depicts the FTIR spectra of these two different types of CQDs. As can be seen from this figure, the H-CQDs show a broad and intensive O-H vibration signals at 3448 cm−1, and stretching mode of CO group around 1680-1720 cm−1, indicating the existence of hydroxyl or carboxyl groups on H-CQD surface [37]. In contrast, the C-CQDs showed relatively weak O-H vibration signals, but intensive C-H stretching signals between 2850 and 3000 cm−1 and vibration of CH3 at 1456 and 1377 cm−1 [40], demonstrate that the C-CQDs have more hydrophobic C-H or CH3 terminal groups. In addition, more intensive CC stretching at 1271 and 1668 cm−1 can be found, suggesting that these synthesized C-CQDs are graphite structure like, corresponding well to the HRTEM results shown in Fig. 1. The photograph of the dispersion of C-CQDs in various organic solvents is shown in the right-bottom of Fig. 2. As can be seen here, the C-CQDs have good solubility in chlorobenzene (CB), 1,2-dichlorobenzene (DCB), and toluene, while poor dispersion in water. This confirms that C-CQDs have more hydrophobic groups at the terminal, which offers better compatibility with polymers in polymer solar cells. To further demonstrate the different surface properties of C-CQDs and H-CQDs, the contact angles of diiodomethane and water on ITO coated with C-CQDs and H-CQDs were tested. As can be seen in the Fig. 3 , the diiodomethane can form a stable droplet on the H-CQD surface, and the contact angel was measured to be 34.11°, while that of C-CQDs is 28.87°. However, the contact angles of water on the surfaces of C-CQDs and H-CQDs were measured to be 34.10° and 28.43°, respectively. These contact angle measurement results further confirmed that C-CQDs show more hydrophobic than H-CQDs.

To further explore the optical properties of C-CQDs, both UV–Vis absorption spectra and the PL spectra of C-CQDs were measured, as shown in Fig. 4 (a) and Fig. 4(b), respectively. As can be seen from the UV–Vis absorption spectra, C-CQDs typically show strong optical absorption in ultraviolet region, with a tail extending out into the visible range. The absorption peak at 301 nm in Fig. 4(a) should be attributed to n-π∗ transition of multi-conjugate CC of the C-CQDs [41,42].

Under 365 nm ultraviolet light irradiation, C-CQDs exhibit bright blue-green emission, while no further passivation step is required for PL to occur, as shown in the photograph of C-CQDs solution (inset of Fig. 4(b)). The PL performance of C-CQDs is the result of interaction through electron transfer or cross-recombination among the C-CQDs in the self-assembled structures. In Fig. 4(b), the emission spectra of C-CQDs show a typical excitation dependent feature as the excitation wavelength exceeds 400 nm [43]. Given these C-CQDs prepared under high temperature, the strong emission of C-CQDs probably comes from the quantum-sized fragment of graphite [21]. Changing the excitation wavelength from 320 to 500 nm with 20 nm steps, the PL peak gradually shifts to longer wavelengths, and the PL intensity increases firstly and then decreases, and reaches the maximum at the excitation of 440 nm. As can be seen in Fig. 4, C-CQDs exhibit typical luminescent excitation-dependent down conversion behavior. Because the emission wavelength range of the C-CQDs is consistent with the region in which P3HT has strong absorption [44,45], the down conversion behavior can be used to increase the absorption of polymer solar cells based on P3HT.

Cyclic voltammetry (CV) measurement was carried out by using a standard three-electrode system to estimate the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) energy levels of C-CQDs. As shown in Fig. 5 , the C-CQDs exhibit apparently a pair of reversible reduction-oxidation peaks, indicating that the C-CQDs is suitable to be used as an acceptor in polymer solar cells. The LUMO and HOMO energy levels were estimated from their reduction potential and oxidation potential according to Equations (1) and (2) [46] as follows:
(1)
LUMO = −
(
E o
red
+ 5.10
)
(
eV
)
(2)
HOMO = −
(
E o
ox
+ 5.10
)
(
eV
)
where E o red and E o ox are the onset reduction potential and onset oxidation potential, respectively. E o red and E o ox were determined to be −1.26 V and 0.25 V, so the corresponding LUMO and HOMO levels are −3.84 eV and −5.35 eV. The E
g of C-CQDs is estimated to be 1.51 eV. The synthesized C-CQDs show low LUMO energy level (E LUMO = −3.84 eV), which is slightly higher than that of the phenyl-C61-butyric acid methyl ester (PC61BM, E LUMO = −4.01 eV) [47], the higher LUMO energy level might result in higher V oc in polymer solar cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of P3HT:C-CQDs based composite films and photovoltaic performance
ce:section.ce:para: It is generally known that PL spectra in conjugated thiophenes arise from radiative recombination of polaron-exciton pairs into Franck-Condon state, and those of C-CQDs are the result of interaction through an electron transfer or cross-recombination among C-CQDs in the self-assembled structures [29,48]. In composite films, a reduction of the PL intensity gives an indication of an effective photo-induced charge transfer from a donor to an acceptor [49,50]. Thus, the PL spectra of P3HT (10 mg/mL), C-CQDs (0.5 mg/mL) and P3HT:C-CQDs (C-CQDs content: 0, 2.5, 5, and 10 wt.% vs. P3HT) composite films were studied to compare the values of their efficiency of photo-induced charge transfer. From Fig. 6 (a), it can be seen that for pure P3HT film strong PL peak occurs between 600 and 750 nm with excitation at 450 nm. While the intensity of PL spectra belonging to P3HT is significantly decreased after C-CQDs are introduced even though with rather small proportion (5%–10%), which suggests an effective photo-induced charge transfer in the composite films. As the proportion of C-CQDs increases, the fluorescence quenching is more obvious, which means more efficient charge separation of the composite films as a result of the increased interfacial areas that facilitate the charge separation within the bulk. What's more, as can be seen in Fig. 6(a), the pure C-CQD film exhibits strong PL peak between 470 and 530 nm, but the PL is quenched efficiently by P3HT in the composite films, indicating the efficiency charge transfer between P3HT and C-CQDs. The PL quenching suggests that C-CQDs may accept electrons efficiently from the P3HT donor polymer, also consistent with the finding in other studies that C-CQDs can be quenched efficiently by either electron acceptor or electron donor [2,51]. The UV–Vis absorption spectra of P3HT and P3HT:C-CQDs (1:5%) composite film in Fig. 6(b) show that after C-CQDs are introduced, there is no significant shift in the absorption spectra. However, there is a slight increase in the absorption intensity for the P3HT:C-CQDs film. This increment should correspond to the light absorption and scattering by well dispersed C-CQDs inside the blend film, which might result in enhanced J sc of the polymer solar cells based on C-CQDs.

Polymer solar cells with a structure of ITO/ZnO/P3HT:C-CQDs/MoO3/Al were fabricated, where P3HT acted as a donor with C-CQDs as an acceptor. The contents of C-CQDs in the active layer were varied as 0, 2.5, 5.0 and 10 wt% (ratio to P3HT), separately. For comparison, devices based on H-CQDs were also fabricated and tested. The layered structure of the device and the corresponding energy level diagram are schematically shown in Fig. 7 . Free holes and electrons can not be created directly in composite films when light is absorbed because excitons have a large binding energy of 0.3 eV, so the energy gaps of electron donors and electron acceptors ΔE1 and ΔE3 should be greater than 0.3 eV [52], where ΔE1 = DLUMO-ALUMO, ΔE3 = DHOMO-AHOMO, D refers to electron donors and A refers to electron acceptors. It has been reported that the HOMO and LUMO values of P3HT are −5.0 and −3.0 eV, respectively [52]. As a result, ΔE1 and ΔE3 of the active layer based on electron-donating P3HT and electron-accepting C-CQDs are 0.35 and 0.84 eV, respectively. Both are greater than the exciton binding energy. Therefore, it is suggested that C-CQDs are suitable to be used as the acceptor materials of P3HT based polymer solar cells.

The photovoltaic performance data of devices are summarized in Table 1 . Fig. 8 (a) shows the J-V curves of the devices based on pure P3HT and P3HT:C-CQDs (1:5%). As expected, both P3HT and P3HT:C-CQDs based devices show excellent diode quality in the dark J-V curves with very low leakage current and high rectification ratio. Upon light illumination, for the device with P3HT-only film as the active layer, an open circuit voltage (V oc) of 0.55 V is accompanied by a short circuit current (J SC) of 0.27 mA/cm2, a fill factor (FF) of 0.52 and a power conversion efficiency (PCE) of 0.08%. The device based on P3HT:H-CQDs (1:5%) shows PCE = 0.10%, V oc = 0.56 V, J sc = 0.36 mA/cm2 and FF = 0.50. In comparison, maximum values of PCE = 0.29%, V oc = 0.62 V, J sc = 0.77 mA/cm2 and FF = 0.60 are obtained for 5 wt% C-CQDs in P3HT. Note that all these devices showed V oc of 0.55–0.63 V, which is reasonable for P3HT based devices [53], while J sc was found to be much lower than that of PC61BM based devices [53], which could be due to unfavorable nano-phase separation (see also the EQE spectrum in Fig. 8(b), and morphology images in Fig. 9 (a), vide infra) and/or lower electron mobility of CQDs. Nevertheless, higher device performance for the P3HT:C-CQDs based device was found in comparison with that with H-CQDs or without CQDs electron acceptor. In addition, higher fill factors (FF) around 0.60 were obtained for all these C-CQDs based devices in comparison with that reported in the literatures [34,35], suggesting more balanced charge transport property in the blended films. These results unambiguously confirmed that CVD is a better method to achieve high performance electron acceptor for use in polymer solar cells. It is worth to note that higher photovoltaic performances of polymer solar cells using CQDs as dopant have been reported in the literatures [32], however, in these devices CQDs function as a dopant in the photoactive layer. The present work confirms that C-CQD could serve as the electron acceptor in polymer solar cells as the potential alternative to PCBM, which is of highly potential since fullerene derivatives are usually of high cost for their complicated synthesis procedures.

Nanophase separation of P3HT:C-CQDs (1:5%) and P3HT:H-CQDs composite films (1:5%) were studied by FETEM, and two typical TEM images are illustrated in Fig. 9. As can be seen from Fig. 9(a), despite large aggregates of C-CQDs (dark spots), reasonable phase separation was formed in the P3HT:C-CQDs composite film, whereas there is no good phase separation was found in the P3HT:H-CQD blended film (Fig. 9(b)), except that large H-CQDs aggregate. Such a nanophase morphological difference could be due to the difference of solubility of different CQDs in hydrophobic DCB solvent. The better phase separation of P3HT:C-CQD film is attributed to good solubility of C-CQDs in DCB, leading to a good dispersion of C-CQDs in the blended film, whereas H-CQDs aggregated together in P3HT matrix due to its poor dispersion in DCB. This result is in good agreement with the FTIR characterization and photovoltaic performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of P3HT:C-CQDs:PC61BM based composite films and photovoltaic performance
ce:section.ce:para:
To further explore the potential of the C-CQDs as photoactive materials, polymer solar cells with a device structure of ITO/ZnO/P3HT:C-CQDs:PC61BM/MoO3/Al were fabricated and tested, where P3HT as the donor, PC61BM as acceptor and C-CQDs with different doping ratio (0, 0.025, 0.05, 0.075 and 0.1 wt%) as doping material. Fig. 10 depicts J-V curves and EQE spectra of P3HT:C-CQDs:PC61BM devices. The key parameters of the best devices are summarized in Table 2 . The P3HT:PC61BM devices without C-CQDs show PCE = 3.30%, V oc = 0.61 V, J sc = 9.02 mA/cm2 and FF = 0.60. In comparison, maximum values of PCE = 3.67%, V oc = 0.61 V, J sc = 9.56 mA/cm2 and FF = 0.63 are obtained from 0.075 wt% C-CQDs in active layer. The improvement of PCE is one hand due to the increase of J
SC , one other hand due to the enhanced FF.

To obtain a deep insight into the working mechanism of C-CQDs in devices, the UV–Vis absorption of P3HT:C-CQDs:PC61BM composite films and impedance spectra of P3HT:C-CQDs:PC61BM devices in the dark were measured. The UV–Vis absorption spectra of P3HT:C-CQDs:PC61BM composite film in Figure S1 show that after C-CQDs are introduced, there is an absorption enhancement for the composite films. This increment should correspond to the light absorption and scattering by well dispersed C-CQDs inside the blend film, which might result in enhanced J sc. The impedance spectra of P3HT:PC61BM solar cells with and without C-CQDs are shown in Figure S2, and the test frequency ranges from 1 Hz to 1.9 MHz. The impedance spectra take the shape of approximate semicircles, and the series resistance of devices is closely related to the diameter of impedance spectra, and larger diameter means lager impedance [54,55]. From the diagram, it is clearly shown that the semicircle's diameter of device without C-CQDs is much larger than that of devices doping C-CQDs, suggesting that introduction of C-CQDs reduced the series resistance, and thus increased the FF and J
SC of devices.

In conclusion, excitation-wavelength-dependent blue-greenish fluorescent C-CQDs were synthesized by CVD. The synthesized C-CQDs have defined graphite structure and show good solubility in common organic solvents, which offers better compatibility with polymers in polymer solar cells. The synthesized C-CQDs show a low LUMO energy level (LUMO = −3.84 eV), which is close to that of PC61BM. Photoluminescence quenching of the P3HT:C-CQDs composite films indicated that photo-induced charge transfer between P3HT and C-CQDs occurs in such a composite film. The performance of devices with C-CQDs as acceptor showed a conversion efficiency of 0.29%, corresponding to more than two times enhancement compared with that (0.08%) of device without using CQD. After doping with C-CQDs, the PCE of P3HT:PC61BM solar cells increased by 11% (3.67% vs. 3.30%). In summary, as environmentally friendly, easily processable, stable and low cost nanomaterials, C-CQDs are of the potential to act as the photoactive materials in polymer solar cells.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (U1610255, U1607120), the Shanxi Provincial Key Innovative Research Team in Science and Technology (2015013002-10, 201605D131045-10), the Shanxi Provincial Key Research and Development Program (201603D111010 and 201703D321015-1) and Key Laboratory of Interface Science and Engineering in Advanced Materials, Ministry of Education, Taiyuan University of Technology (KLISEAM201502).

ce:section.ce:para:

figs1
figs2
Impedance spectra of P3HT:PC61BM cells with or without C-CQDs (0.075%) in the dark.
figs2

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.optmat.2017.10.010.
